sentences.text: Yes.
[SIGHS] Oh, by the-- around the end of 1940 or beginning '41.
I think it the in 1940, if I'm not mistaken.
No.
It was like almost inside.
In other words, the front-- the front entrance went into the marketplace.
But there was a back entrance too that went into the ghetto.
So we could stay in the apartment.
Yes.
Well, you couldn't-- you couldn't get out where they announced that this is the ghetto.
It comprises this and this streets.

And if they would catch you, they will shot you or arrest you, you know, because you were wearing armbands, you know, or your-- on your arm with a Star of David in the middle of [INAUDIBLE] Judah, mean Jew.
So you were afraid to-- to go behind that.
There were no barriers or-- or chains, nothing.
It was open.
But nobody would-- would dare to-- to go out there because not only you were afraid for the German, but you were afraid for the Poles too.
They were not better from the Germans.
So if they would see you, they may report you to the Germans.
I don't remember.
I don't-- I don't think so.
No, they were not allowed to, because they didn't want to let them in, because they would do all kind of business with the Jews, you know?
Well, the restriction where that there was a certain time where you couldn't go out on the street anymore in the evening.
I don't remember what time it was.
And you had to report to the same where you had to report to for work.
And you had to only buy in Jewish stores.

And other restriction where you were afraid to walk too much on the street because the SS men may just came in and-- and-- and arrest some-- some people, you know, and you would never see them anymore.
Oh, yeah.
It happened to people we knew-- I knew, yeah.
Disappear, you know?
You-- you never know what's happened to them.
In the beginning, you could if you had money.
You could buy it, naturally.
Yeah.
There were Jewish stores, and you could-- you could buy food, yeah.
There were restriction on it.
There many time, you know, you had to stay in lines to get the food.
And if you were in a long line, by the time you got to-- to the store, there wasn't any-- anything-- anymore left, bread or-- or milk, or whatever you wanted to buy.
Well, as before, before the-- they closed the ghetto, the business was still open.
In other words, we-- we still had the business, you know?
But once the-- they closed the ghetto, they send the Jews to the ghetto, we had to abandon everything in the house, in the-- in the-- in the yard, and everything.
Ah, sure.
Not only our-- my family, every Jew who ever had any businesses.
Well, I imagine they still-- they have some money, you know, accumulated from before, you know?
See, they-- they-- most of the Jewish stores, even before the ghetto, they closed down.
See, there was closed by-- by us in the [GERMAN], a big like a supermarket.
At that time, there was no supermarket.
So they closed that store, I think, maybe a week or two weeks after the German came in.
And they put in a sign on the store.
What I going to say it in Polish.
[INAUDIBLE] you're going to translate.
[SPEAKING POLISH] That mean, here were in that store was a-- a man who was cheating the Poles, and he was sent out to that town, [INAUDIBLE].
Well, I was-- I was sent still when there was the-- the ghetto in Bochnia.
I was sent to work in a town by the name Klaj.
That was by-- 15 kilometers from Bochnia.
And we used to travel there by train every morning, early in the morning, and came back in the evening.
And that-- that was like a forest.
But the Polish army had there a munitions there.
And we were working there.
In other words, they used to came trains with ammunition.
And we used to sort them from caliber, you know, the sizes.
And down used to come German trucks.
And we used to load the trucks.
And they used to go that time already to the Russians front.
No.
We-- we didn't get nothing.
We had to take our-- our lunch from home.
No.
No, they did not pay.
No.
There was-- there was in the beginning.
Before I used to go there, they-- we used to work for-- for Germans.
They used to pay us something.
But after that, they didn't pay.
Now, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] manage what to do.

A lot-- a lot of people were afraid to go to the synagogues, because they could, you know, during the services, you know, come in and-- and-- and take out some people, and send them away, but nobody knows where.
Right away, there were-- there were two or three-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] three times where they send away a lot of the Jews from the ghetto.
In other words, the ghetto was smaller and smaller and smaller, less and less people.
Well, by that time when they started to liquidate, little by little, the ghettos, I was already in Klaj.
In that place, they built barracks from us.
And we used to stay there overnight.

But they used to-- you see, that Judenrat, you know, they're what the-- the-- the-- the people who are the-- the heads of the-- of the ghetto, they got orders from the German, by tomorrow, you have to bring in 10,000 Jews or 5,000 or whatever.
And they had to-- to-- to bring that people to a certain place.
And they sent them away.
So there was 10,000 people less in the ghetto, because in the ghetto, was a lot of people, a lot of Jews, because they were brought, you know, from the surrounding vicinities, you know?
They were small towns, a lot of small towns around Bochnia.
And they all were brought into the ghetto in Bochnia.
And every time after they send away in the ghetto was smaller and smaller, in other words, less and less people.
And I remember even when my mother and my sister, she was sent out, I think, with the third one.
And she went to Belzec.
And my uncle still left in the ghetto.
He was still in the ghetto because he was working for the-- for the Germans in a fur business.
So the Germany needed fur, you know, for the soldiers.
And I was that time already in Klaj.
But when it came the last-- last what they called an [GERMAN], you know-- in German, they call it [GERMAN], last transport-- that time they liquidated completely the-- the ghetto.
There was no more ghetto.
And by that time, they even sent us away from Klaj, and we went to a-- to a camp, but by name Plaszow.
Approximately.
I think it was the third time when they were, you know, cutting the ghetto, sending away people.
It was smaller and smaller.
Well, in the beginning, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] oh, no.
See, we-- we could come once every two weeks, we could come back to Bochnia for a weekend.
But by that time, when they allowed us to come, my mother wasn't there anymore.
She was gone.
She was sent away already.
Because when I got to Bochnia, the apartment was empty.
Nobody was home.
So I remember after that, I didn't want-- want to anymore come.
So I remember, I stayed with my uncle that time.
In his apartment, he had one room.
He was staying with seven, eight people.
So they did not even-- didn't even have room for me where to sleep, you know?
So after that, I didn't-- you could still come to Bochnia, but I didn't want to come anymore because there was nobody there, and it was nobody to come to.
You mentioned that people from the surrounding areas were all Jews were sent into the ghetto--
Mhm.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
We had-- we had one room and a kitchen.
And in the kitchen, there was another family living, yes.
In other words, we had the bedroom, and they had the kitchen.
No.
They were people from-- from another town.
They went not even from the surrounding area.
They were far, far away in-- from Poland.
You didn't have no choice.
They didn't-- you couldn't choose whom you want to have there.
But to leave it here, the Judenrat, they-- they assigned people that you have to go live here and here.
Sure, it was difficult, you know?
First of all, you had-- we had to cross through the kitchen where I used to live.
And, you know, you do not have no privacy, you know?
We lived-- me, and my mother, and sister lived in the one room.
And it was a hardship, naturally.
No, we didn't build them.
It's possible-- I think the barracks were still from the Poles, from the Polish army.
No, there was three barracks.
And that's where we-- we stay in this barracks.
No, we didn't build them.
Well, barracks, there was-- there was one-- one bed on top of the other one, you know?
How you call that?
Bunk bed, yes.
Right.
And one barrack could be 60 people, maybe more.
And we got there, there was a kitchen.
We got a-- during lunch time, they gave us a soup, and in the morning, a coffee with a piece of bread.
And-- and that's what it was.
It wasn't enough, no.
I was always hungry.
That was my biggest problem.
Hunger, ah, it's very hard to describe what means hunger.
Hunger is a terrible thing.
But what could you do?
If you got a piece of bread, you-- that you had to have it for a whole day.
So you just grabbed a piece of it.
And you say the rest is for later.
But later, you took another piece because you couldn't [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] control yourself, you know?
So it came in the morning.
You didn't have nothing because you-- you ate it up, you know?
You had to wait till-- till the following day till you got another piece of bread.
And especially, when you are that time 17 or 18-- 18 years old, and you're tall, and skinny, and you need to-- you need nourishment, you know?
Was there any--
And that was only in 1940.
By the end of '41, that wasn't-- the bad time didn't come yet.
The worst time came after that.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, sure.
If you had-- had left a piece of bread, you put it under your hat.
So in case somebody wants to steal it, you can feel it, you know?
Oh, sure.
I don't know.
After when we were living in the barracks, I-- we were there just only, I think, a few months.
Because down it came the last-- the last transport was sent out from Bochnia.
They liquidated the ghetto in Bochnia.
So after liquidated the ghetto in Bochnia, they send us away from Klaj too.
We are working there, and physically, it wasn't so bad in-- in-- in-- Klaj, you know?
There was nobody shot or killed, you know?
No.
I didn't even know where they were sent away.
We only knew that that was-- they were sent-- deported.
But you didn't know where they were deported.
I think I didn't find that out till after the war where they went.
He-- he was still in Bochnia.
But I don't know what happened to him.
I have no idea.
I imagine they were sent to Belzec, too-- to the last transport that was-- was deported, I think, to Belzec, too.
But I have-- couldn't help.
But no confirmation, and there was nobody left from the family there that you could ask or find out or could who would know anything about it, because I was left alone, you know?
I had no family, though.
Well, some people had some connection with the-- in the-- with the people in the kitchen could get some extra food, naturally, could get an extra-- extra plate of soup, you know?
But I did not have connection, you know?
I didn't know the people, you know?
So I couldn't get extra food.
And I remember one time, I got sick there being in Klaj.
And the ghetto was still-- there was still a ghetto in Bochnia.
And I got the-- the jaundice.
Jaundice is it?
[CRYING] I was all yellow, you know?
So they send me-- they send me back to Bochnia to a hospital.
But I went to Bochnia, and I didn't want to go to the hospital, because if I would go to the hospital, I wouldn't be here anymore.
So I remember I stayed with my uncle.
He was still in the ghetto that time.
So I stayed with him for three weeks.
And I remember I couldn't eat nothing.
I didn't no medication, no nothing, because I was afraid to go to the hospital to get medication.
And then after three weeks, I felt better, and I went back to Klaj.
The hospital that you're talking about, was that inside the ghetto?
Yes.
Because a lot of people were killed in the hospitals, you know?
They didn't help you.
They didn't treat you.
I mean, I mean not killed by shotting or something.
They didn't shot them.
But they didn't treat them, so they die, you know, from the sickness, whatever they had it, you know, because they didn't medicate you, you know?
There was medication for-- for jaundice, you know, or other things.
But they didn't give you that.
We knew when that day when the ghetto was liquidated because the rumors, you know, it's not for [INAUDIBLE].
And I remember the day when they said today that the ghetto is being liquidated, and everybody is being sent away, deported.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that day, even the German soldiers that was that time, there was no-- no SS that time.
There was Wehrmacht, regular German soldiers.
They were very nice.
I can't complain against them.
They-- they knew about that too.
So they didn't take care if you did work or didn't work, you know?
They were sympathetic to you.
But after that, a few days later, we're getting ready to go-- to go to work, because the barrack filled like fume that feed from-- from the place where we worked, you know?
Suddenly, We see trucks arriving in front of the barracks.
We saw something is brewing, something.
We didn't know what's going on.
And suddenly, they tell you, everybody out and on the trucks.
And that's when I took us to-- to Plaszow.
Now, were you able to-- to take any possessions with you, or--
Yes, you could take-- you could take everything you had with you.
But the minute you arrived in Plaszow, you have to take out everything, and they took away everything.
Mm, not really.
Later on in-- when I was other camp, I-- I had a close friend who are living in Israel.
But from-- from Klaj, I don't remember, though.
Came and they took us to Plaszow.
Oh, a few hours.
I wasn't so far.
It was like from Klaj to-- Plaszow was like distance from-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] from Bochnia to Cracow was 40 kilometers.
And that was suburbs of-- of Cracow.
So it could be another 5 kilometer.
It wasn't so far.
It took maybe a couple hours.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
On-- when we got-- the first thing when we got into Plaszow, naturally, we had to-- to-- to leave it on the floor, everything we had with us.
They-- they allow you still to take some-- some personal things, they still allow you to take.
But most importantly, [INAUDIBLE] you have money or jewelry, you know, and if you say no, and they looked, and they-- they shot you on the spot, you know?
But right-- the same-- the first day when we came into Plaszow, so they didn't took us to barracks.
They took us what they call it an appeals place, in other words, like the a-- a assembly place.
And there were already thousands of people assembled there.
And when we coming there, they were hanging three people, three young boys where they were hung that time.
Later on, we found out the reason they were hung up because they were whistling a Russian song.
That is a very popular song, 'Volga Volga,' [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH].
That means Volga is the Russian, you know, river.
And that's why they-- they hung them.
Because, you see, the-- the guards in Plaszow were Ukrainian, but they were many time worse than Germans.
And they understand Russian.
And that's-- that was the first day, our reception in Plaszow.
And down they sent us in barracks.
And the following day, they-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they make groups, you know, for-- for 10 or 15 people and were sending us to work, you know?
And when we-- we arrived in-- in Plaszow, that must have be [INAUDIBLE] end '42 of beginning '43.
I-- could be beginning of '43.
Wintertime?
Pardon me?
No, it was springtime.
But I can't remember springtime, because that time, they deported all the Jews from the Krakau ghetto.
